_title: IEC 60617 Part 7 — Switchgear, controlgear and protective devices

symbols.usage_notes: Make contact — normally open. Closes when operated. The moving contact (diagonal blade) and fixed contact (vertical bar) are shown in the open position.
Break contact — normally closed. Opens when operated. Shown in the closed position with a diagonal stroke indicating NC behaviour.
Single-pole double-throw contact. Common terminal switches between NO and NC. Used in selector circuits, indication, control logic.
Generic switch — single-pole single-throw. Use when device specifics are not yet determined. For an isolating switch, prefer SWITCH_ISOLATING.
Single-pole switch — most basic mechanical switch.

2-pole switch-disconnector (load break and isolation). Used as main isolator on single-phase distribution boards. Distinct from ISOLATOR_2P — switch-disconnector can break load current, plain isolator cannot.
3-pole switch-disconnector. Used as the main isolator on 3-phase distribution boards. Mechanically linked operation.
4-pole switch-disconnector — switches L1, L2, L3 and N. Used where switched neutral is required (IT systems, generator/utility transfer).
Bus section switch — connects two halves of a split busbar. Used in main switchboards with dual incomers for source-changeover / N+1 topology.
2-pole isolator (no load break). For visible isolation only — must be operated off-load. Horizontal bar across the top indicates isolator (no quick-break mechanism).
3-pole isolator for 3-phase circuits. Off-load only.
4-pole isolator. Off-load only.
1-pole miniature circuit breaker for final circuits. Annotate rated current (In), trip curve (B/C/D), and breaking capacity (Icu). Standard final-circuit protective device.
2-pole MCB — breaks both line and neutral simultaneously. Required for IT systems, some EV charge points. Mechanically linked operation.
3-pole MCB for 3-phase final circuits. Mechanically linked operation.
4-pole MCB — breaks L1, L2, L3 and N. Used where switched neutral is required (IT systems, source changeover circuits).
Moulded case circuit breaker — typically 100A to 1600A. Adjustable thermal (Ir) and magnetic (Im) trip settings. Annotate trip unit type (thermal-magnetic or electronic).
4-pole MCCB — for circuits requiring switched neutral or 4-pole isolation (TN-S with neutral isolation, generator/utility transfer).

Air circuit breaker — typically 800A and above, used as the incomer on main LV switchboards. Drawn similarly to MCCB but with a double internal bar to distinguish. Annotate trip unit type and short-time delay settings.
4-pole ACB — incomer with switched neutral. Common on main LV boards with multiple supply sources.

1-pole fuse — rectangle with leads. Annotate fuse rating and type (gG general purpose, aM motor circuit, gF fast-acting, BS88 etc.). Common at the supply intake (BS88 cut-out) and on final circuits where MCBs are not used.
Three single-pole fuses in a 3-phase circuit, drawn aligned. Use for fused supply intake or as backup protection.
Combined fuse and switch — switch breaks load, fuse provides backup protection. Common as a main isolator on industrial DBs. Annotate fuse and switch ratings.
3-pole contactor for motor control. Coil shown separately. Small circle at top indicates the automatic-return spring. Annotate AC3 rating (motor switching).
Three contactors arranged for star-delta motor starting (main, star, delta). Drawn as a single composite symbol. See MOTOR_STARTER_SD for the complete starter symbol.
Relay / contactor coil — rectangle with vertical bar. Annotate coil voltage (e.g. 230V AC, 24V DC) and tag. Pairs with contacts elsewhere on the schematic.
Thermal overload relay — installed in series with contactor for motor protection. Zigzag indicates thermal bimetal element. Annotate set current range and trip class (10, 20, 30).
Motor protection circuit breaker — combines short-circuit (magnetic) and overload (thermal) protection in one device. Standard final-circuit motor protection up to ~32A. Annotate set current.
2-pole RCD — no overload protection (must be paired with an MCB upstream). Internal toroid symbol (figure-of-eight). Annotate sensitivity (30mA standard for socket outlets) and type (AC, A, F, B).
4-pole RCD for 3-phase + N circuits. Required for 3-phase final circuits with shock protection (workshops, kitchens, EV chargers).

symbols.iec_description: Residual current circuit-breaker with overcurrent protection — 1P+N
symbols.usage_notes: Combined MCB + RCD in a single device — 1P+N width. Standard for final circuits requiring both overcurrent and earth fault protection (UK domestic since 18th Edition).
2-pole RCBO — switches both L and N. Required for IT systems and some EV circuits.
Type 1 SPD — diamond shape with earth lead. Tests using lightning impulse (10/350μs). Required where LPS is installed or for buildings in high lightning exposure. Annotate Iimp, Up, Uc.
Type 2 SPD — smaller diamond. Tests using 8/20μs nominal. Required at main DB in all new installations (IEC 60364-4-44 AMD2:2018). Annotate In, Up, Uc.
Type 3 SPD — point-of-use protection for sensitive equipment. Lowest let-through voltage (Up ≤ 1.5kV typically). Annotate Up.

Automatic transfer switch — selects between two power sources (typically utility and generator). Two inputs, one output. Annotate rated current and transfer time. Internal logic controls source selection.
Three-way ATS — selects between three sources (e.g. utility, generator, UPS). Used in critical applications (data centres, hospitals).
Manual transfer switch / changeover switch — operator selects source. Used for small installations or as bypass for an ATS.
Earth leakage relay — sensing only, must be paired with a separate switching device (contactor or shunt-trip MCCB). Used on larger circuits where a single RCCB rating is not available.
Mechanically interlocked pair of contacts — both cannot be closed simultaneously. Vertical line indicates the interlock. Used between source ATS contactors (utility/generator) to prevent paralleling.
Auxiliary contact — derived from a power contactor or breaker. NO state. Used for control logic, indication. Annotate the parent device tag.
Auxiliary contact, NC state. Often used for fault/trip signalling from a breaker.
Push-to-make pushbutton — momentary NO contact. Vertical line indicates the plunger. Used for start, reset, accept commands.
Push-to-break pushbutton — momentary NC contact. Used for stop, cancel commands.
Pushbutton with integral lamp — combines a contact and an indicator. Annotate lamp colour and function.
Emergency stop — NC contact with mushroom-head latching pushbutton. Drawn with a circle above the contact symbol. Required at machinery to ISO 13850.
Key-operated switch — switch with a key symbol on top. Used for secure or critical operations (e.g. lift maintenance, hospital theatre changeover).
Two-position rotary selector. Common for hand/off, manual/auto.
Three-position rotary selector. Common for hand/off/auto, forward/off/reverse.
On-delay contact — delays closing after operation. Curve symbol indicates the timing function. Annotate delay duration.
Off-delay contact — delays opening after de-energising. Mirror curve symbol. Annotate delay duration.
Qualifying overlay indicating a switching device has a manual external handle. Often combined with SWITCH_DISCONNECTOR or ISOLATOR.
Current-limiting device — fuse-like rectangle with internal bars. Used to reduce let-through energy at high PSCC.
Voltage limiter — diamond with earth lead. Generic form for an MOV or gas discharge tube. SPD_TYPE2 is the formal IEC type-classified form.
Overcurrent protection relay — rectangle with internal indication. Used on MV switchgear, large MCCBs with separate trip units.
Undervoltage relay — used for automatic disconnection on supply loss (e.g. generator changeover trigger, motor undervoltage trip).
Differential protection relay — compares current in two CTs (in/out). Used for transformer differential, busbar protection.
Direct-on-line motor starter — composite symbol for contactor + overload relay. Annotate motor rating. Simplest motor control; high inrush current.
Star-delta starter for reduced-inrush motor starting. Suitable for motors > 7.5 kW where DOL inrush is too high. Annotate transition time.
VFD-based motor starter — soft starting plus variable speed. Standard for HVAC fans, pumps. Annotate speed range.
Pressure-operated switch — dome symbol above the contact represents the pressure sensor. Annotate setpoint.
Thermostat — rectangle symbol above the contact represents the temperature sensor. Used for HVAC controls, motor temperature trip.
Float switch — bar symbol above the contact represents the float. Used in sump pumps, water tanks.
Level switch — generic (capacitive, ultrasonic, conductive). Distinct from FLOAT which is the mechanical/buoyancy form.
Generic circuit-breaker box. Use only when device class (MCB/MCCB/ACB) is not yet decided. Otherwise prefer the specific class symbol.
Generic single-pole disconnector. Used when pole count is not yet fixed. Horizontal bar indicates 'no load' (isolator) operation.
Generic single-pole contactor. The small circle indicates the spring-return / power-operated behaviour (distinct from a manual switch).
Generic relay symbol — rectangle without specific function marking. Annotate function (overcurrent, undervoltage, etc.).
Switch certified for isolation duty (e.g. MAINS marked). Long horizontal bar over the contact indicates the isolation rating. Used as functional switch + isolator.
Bolted/removable link — a non-switching connection that can be unbolted for maintenance. Two terminals connected by a bar with knobs. Used on PE-N links in TN-C-S systems.
